turns.content: Hey, I've been reading about passing strings to C from Rust for a few weeks now. I think I understand the general idea of using pointers and null termination but I still can't get my head around why the standard string types behave so differently when crossing that boundary.

It's a common stumbling block because in scripting languages you often treat strings as opaque blobs, but Rust forces you to track ownership. The key distinction here is between CStr and CString. When you call into a C function expecting a null-terminated string, you pass an immutable reference to the data using the AsRef trait on str which creates a borrow without copying. That's what CStr does—it wraps existing bytes as a read-only view so no allocation happens at the boundary. If you try to write to it or extend it after passing it across the FFI, you'll panic because that ownership has already been transferred implicitly by the pointer.

Ah okay so I shouldn't use CString in those cases then? But my code currently uses CString::new and passes that directly into a C function expecting a char pointer. The compiler is screaming at me about dangling references though.

That's exactly the wrong assumption you made early on. You cannot pass a CString which owns its buffer across an FFI boundary because it requires interior mutability or explicit drop logic in the callee that Rust won't allow without unsafe blocks. The CString type is designed for when you need to own the data locally, but once you cross into C land, you must convert it to a CStr first using the from trait which creates a zero-cost borrow of the underlying bytes. Try replacing your direct pass with c_str() on the CString and ensure the target function accepts an argument that implements AsRef<str>.
